message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Over the course of the CoVID-19 pandemic, we utilized widely-available real-time data to create models for predicting its spread, and to estimate the time evolution for each of the USA CoVID-19 waves. Our recent
                  <jats:italic>medrxiv</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>org</jats:italic>
                  preprint (
                  <jats:italic>10</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>1101_2021</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>08</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>16</jats:italic>
                  .
                  <jats:italic>21262150</jats:italic>
                  ) examined the USA Summer 2021 resurgence, from ∼6/7/2021 up through ∼8/15/2021 (
                  <jats:italic>Stage 1</jats:italic>
                  ). Our preprint covering this period showed that CoVID-19 could infect virtually all susceptible non-vaccinated persons, who were practicing minimal
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  and NO
                  <jats:italic>Mask-Wearing</jats:italic>
                  .
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  The most recent USA Summer 2021 resurgence data, from ∼8/13/2021 up through 10/7/2021 (
                  <jats:italic>Stage 2</jats:italic>
                  ), shows a significant “flattening of the curve”. Since no new government mandates were involved, our interpretation is that some vaccine-hesitant people have now elected to become vaccinated. The
                  <jats:italic>Social Distancing</jats:italic>
                  parameter in our model showed a ∼6.67X increase between
                  <jats:italic>Stage 1</jats:italic>
                  and
                  <jats:italic>Stage 2</jats:italic>
                  , indicating that this parameter also can serve as an indicator of vaccination rates. The other parameter in our model, which is associated with
                  <jats:italic>Mask-Wearing</jats:italic>
                  , increased from zero to a finite but relatively small value. Using the 10/7/2021 USA CoVID-19 overall mortality rate of ∼1.60942 % gives these updated predictions for the total number of USA CoVID-19 cases and deaths:
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>TOTAL</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (3
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  21
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  2022) ≈ 52, 188, 000 ;
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>Deaths</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (3
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  21
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  2022) ≈ 839, 900,
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>TOTAL</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (3
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  21
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  2024) ≈ [52, 787, 000 ;
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>Deaths</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (3
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  21
                  <jats:italic>/</jats:italic>
                  2024) ≈ 849, 600, assuming no new 2021 Winter Resurgence occurs (
                  <jats:italic>with 3 Figures</jats:italic>
                  ).
                </jats:p>
title: Updated Model for the USA Summer 2021 CoVID-19 Resurgence

reference.unstructured: https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.05.04.20091207v1 “Initial Model for the Impact of Social Distancing on CoVID-19 Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.06.30.20143149v1 “Orthogonal Functions for Evaluating Social Distancing Impact on CoVID-19 Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2020.08.07.20169904 “Model to Describe Fast Shutoff of CoVID-19 Pandemic Spread”
https://medrxiv.org/cgi/content/short/2021.08.16.21262150 “The IHME vs Me: Modeling USA CoVID-19 Spread, Early Data to the Fifth Wave”
